did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel hybrid deep learning model for skin cancer detection. The method combines Xception for feature extraction, Squeeze and Excitation Network (SENet) for feature enhancement, and a Transformer module for classification. It was evaluated on benign and malignant dermoscopic images from the Kaggle dataset, achieving an accuracy of 96.83%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel hybrid deep learning model for skin cancer detection, detailing original experimental work including the development of a specific architecture (Xception, SENet, Transformer), data preprocessing, training on the Kaggle dataset, and comparative evaluation against other models (MobileNet, VGG-16) with reported metrics (96.83% accuracy). It is published in a conference proceedings (IEEE IPAS 2025) and contains original results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Novel Hybrid Deep Learning Model for Enhancing Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2025 IEEE 6th International Conference on Image Processing, Applications and Systems (IPAS)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel hybrid deep learning model for automated skin cancer detection using dermoscopic images. The architecture integrates Xception for feature extraction, Squeeze and Excitation Network (SENet) for feature enhancement, and a Transformer module for classification. The model was trained and evaluated on a balanced Kaggle dataset containing 3,297 benign and malignant skin lesion images. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed network outperforms baseline models like MobileNet, Xception, and VGG-16, achieving an accuracy of 96.83%, recall of 97%, precision of 96.67%, and F1 score of 96.83%. The findings indicate that combining these specific deep learning components effectively enhances detection performance for clinical applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes used in the Kaggle dataset for skin cancer detection. The dataset contained 1,440 raw benign images (balanced to 1,890 via augmentation). In the test set, 300 benign images were used, with 291 correctly identified as True Negatives (TN) and 10 incorrectly predicted as False Positives (FP).

The other binary class in the Kaggle dataset. The dataset contained 1,197 raw malignant images (balanced to 1,890 via augmentation). In the test set, 300 malignant images were used, with 290 correctly identified as True Positives (TP) and 9 incorrectly predicted as False Negatives (FN). The paper explicitly links malignant lesions to melanoma in the introduction and references.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign and malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin cancer binary classification' using a Transformer module for classification. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Benign vs. Malignant) from dermoscopic images. The output is evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, F1 score, and a confusion matrix (TP, TN, FP, FN). The loss function used is cross-entropy, which is standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset (Malignant vs. Benign)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset collected from Kaggle, originally sourced from the ISIC Archive. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions categorized as benign or malignant. The raw dataset was imbalanced and underwent preprocessing (resizing, grayscale conversion, hair removal via black-hat transformation) and data augmentation (flip, rotate, zoom) to balance the classes.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid deep learning model (Xception, SENet, Transformer) with 10 epochs, batch size 32, and Adam optimizer.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process to monitor loss and accuracy.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance, resulting in an accuracy of 96.83%.
use.other: The raw dataset was augmented to balance the class distribution before splitting into training, validation, and testing sets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed model and baselines were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, recall, precision, F1 score) and an ablation study, without statistical significance testing.

preprocessing: Data augmentation (flip, rotate, zoom) to balance classes

proposed_network.architecture: Hybrid Deep Learning Model (Xception + SENet + Transformer)

Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset (Malignant vs.notes: The proposed network significantly outperformed the transfer learning models (MobileNet, Xception, VGG-16) in terms of accuracy, recall, precision, and F1 score. All models were trained, validated, and tested with the same data and identical configurations.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Deep Learning Model (Xception + SENet + Transformer)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; described as a novel hybrid model combining Xception, SENet, and Transformer modules.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (resized from original Kaggle dataset images)

architecture_details.feature_extractor: Xception network (36 convolutional layers, 14 modules with residual connections, Depth-wise Separable convolutions)
architecture_details.feature_enhancement: Squeeze and Excitation Network (SENet) for channel attention and feature recalibration

classification_or_output_head.activation: Implied softmax/probability score (0-1) via cross-entropy loss

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning model, which integrates Xception for feature extraction, Squeeze and Excitation Network (SENet) for feature enhancement, and a Transformer module for classification, effectively addresses challenges in skin cancer detection such as imbalanced datasets and visual obstructions like hair.
The proposed network significantly outperformed baseline transfer learning models (MobileNet, Xception, and VGG-16) in binary classification of benign and malignant skin lesions.
The model achieved a testing accuracy of 96.83%, recall of 97%, precision of 96.67%, and F1 score of 96.83% on the Kaggle dataset, demonstrating high diagnostic performance.

The combination of Xception, SENet, and Transformer modules provides a promising computer-aided diagnostic tool that can assist dermatologists in identifying skin cancer with minimal effort and high accuracy.

The study acknowledges that manual skin cancer inspection is susceptible to human error and that early detection is critical for improving survival rates, highlighting the clinical potential of the proposed automated system.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on a single Kaggle dataset (derived from ISIC) for both training and testing, with no external validation on independent clinical datasets, limiting generalizability.
The dataset was artificially balanced via augmentation (flip, rotate, zoom) to 1,890 samples per class, which may not reflect real-world class imbalance distributions in clinical practice.
Preprocessing included hair removal via black-hat transformation and grayscale conversion, which may discard color information critical for dermatological diagnosis.
The model was trained for only 10 epochs, which is a very short training duration for deep learning models, potentially leading to underfitting or insufficient convergence.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the performance differences between the proposed model and baselines.
The paper acknowledges that the model has not yet been deployed in clinical settings or validated with additional datasets, representing a gap between experimental results and clinical relevance.
Data leakage risk exists as the Kaggle dataset is publicly available and widely used; the model may have overfit to specific artifacts or biases present in this specific public collection.
